scenario_results.reason: Normal in-scope execution advanced through explicit lifecycle states and closed with traceable commercial closeout and turnover evidence.
Missing jurisdiction, permit, approved-plan, and authority context forced blocked status and low-confidence evidence requests.
Mid-process artifact gaps preserved hold or rework state instead of allowing silent progression.
Scope mismatch and unresolved change handling were routed into exception and rework logic rather than absorbed into normal execution.
Completion without photos, tests, punch closure, and final inspection evidence was refused with a named evidence-gap package.
Permit-entire and site-readiness blockers kept the package from advancing and triggered authority-safe escalation.
Inspection, objection, and turnover blockers prevented substantial completion posture until closeout evidence was complete.
Code interpretation and trade signoff were refused and routed to the correct licensed authority.
New safety and inspection failures reversed prior readiness and reopened the exception queue.
Change-order approval and subcontractor commercial acceptance were routed out of lane with only execution-impact context retained.
Conflicting public and tenant occupancy or closeout rules escalated cleanly without fabricated precedence.
Tenant adaptation narrowed packaging and lifecycle handling while preserving public-baseline permit, inspection, safety, and occupancy guardrails.
Commercial construction terminology was normalized into the correct artifact set before a lifecycle decision was made.

notes: Scenario set covers normal execution, blocked prerequisites, mid-process artifact gaps, rework exceptions, unsupported completion, permit blockers, inspection and closeout blockers, authority refusal, terminology handling, and tenant adaptation.

Functionality mapping and scenario coverage include all shared abilities, all execution-processing-and-fulfillment abilities, and all construction-and-field-services abilities required by the audit contract.
The package is validated as a spec pack but remains specification-based until a retained implementation is promoted separately.
